title: Thus Spake Zarathustra: A Book for All and None
author: Nietzsche, Friedrich Wilhelm
summary: Thus Spake Zarathustra presents Nietzsche’s prophetic figure Zarathustra, a creation embodying his lifelong ideals: the Superman, the transvaluation of values, and the will to power. Written in poetic prose, the book critiques Christianity, modern decadence, and democratic equality, urging a revaluation that elevates strong, life-affirming types. Mrs. Forster-Nietzsche’s introduction traces Zarathustra’s genesis in Nietzsche’s notes, linking sources such as The Gay Science and earlier essays. Themes include great healthiness, eternal recurrence, and cultural renewal rooted in a modified Graeco-Roman vitality. Zarathustra’s speeches and parables exhort readers to overcome sameness, embrace nobility, and create new values in vivid, poetic, prophetic voice.

characters.name: Zarathustra
characters.lifespan: literary protagonist; presented as Nietzsche's ideal-figure who preaches and endures through his discourses

characters.name: Mrs Forster-Nietzsche
characters.personality: explanatory, biographical, reverent toward her brother
characters.lifespan: introducer of the edition; appears in the printed introduction recounting Zarathustra's genesis

characters.name: Friedrich Nietzsche
characters.age: middle-aged to late (authorial presence in introduction)
characters.personality: intense, visionary, contrarian thinker (recounted by his sister)
characters.lifespan: author and historical person referenced; in text his creative acts produce Zarathustra

characters.name: The Superman
characters.lifespan: an ideal projected by Zarathustra and Nietzsche rather than a historical person

characters.name: The Saviour
characters.personality: religious, redemptive figure (criticised in the text)
characters.lifespan: used as an evaluative standard that Nietzsche critiques and re-evaluates

characters.name: Argonauts
characters.personality: adventurous, ideal-seeking explorers (metaphor for seekers of the ideal)
characters.lifespan: metaphorical collective used in the Gay Science quotation within the introduction

characters.name: Persians
characters.personality: invoked as historical precursors and broad-view historians
characters.lifespan: referenced historically; used as cultural exemplars in the introduction

characters.name: Greeks
characters.lifespan: referred to historically as a culture that produced many great individuals

characters.name: Schopenhauer
characters.personality: philosophical precursor referenced in Nietzsche's essays
characters.lifespan: mentioned as part of Nietzsche's earlier intellectual context

characters.name: Philologists (We Philologists)
characters.lifespan: appears as a referenced collective in quotations from Nietzsche's earlier writings
